content:
# Genesis Chapter 42 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Translator:** SOPHIA (Consensus of EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 25r–25v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_042_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_042_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 42–43

---

*Jacob Sends Ten Brothers to Egypt for Grain (vv. 1–5)*

**42:1** And Jacob saw that there was grain in Egypt, and Jacob said to his sons, "Why do you look at one another?" **42:2** And he said, "Behold, I have heard that there is grain in Egypt. Go down there and buy for us from there, that we may live and not die."

**42:3** And Joseph's ten brothers went down to buy grain from Egypt. **42:4** But Benjamin, Joseph's brother, Jacob did not send with his brothers, for he said, "Lest harm befall him." **42:5** And the sons of Israel came to buy among those who came, for the famine was in the land of Canaan.

---

*The Dream Fulfilled — Joseph Recognizes His Brothers (vv. 6–9)*

**42:6** Now Joseph was the ruler over the land — he was the one who sold to all the people of the land. And Joseph's brothers came and bowed down to him with their faces to the ground.

**42:7** And Joseph saw his brothers and recognized them, but he made himself unrecognizable to them and spoke harshly with them and said to them, "From where do you come?" And they said, "From the land of Canaan, to buy food."

**42:8** And Joseph recognized his brothers, but they did not recognize him. **42:9** And Joseph remembered the dreams that he had dreamed about them, and he said to them, "You are spies — to see the nakedness of the land you have come."

---

*Joseph Accuses Them — The Test Proposed (vv. 10–17)*

**42:10** And they said to him, "No, my lord — your servants have come to buy food. **42:11** We are all sons of one man. We are honest men — your servants have never been spies."

**42:12** And he said to them, "No — you have come to see the nakedness of the land."

**42:13** And they said, "Your servants are twelve brothers, sons of one man in the land of Canaan. And behold — the youngest is with our father today, and the one is no more."

**42:14** And Joseph said to them, "It is as I said to you — you are spies. **42:15** By this you shall be tested: by the life of Pharaoh, you shall not go out from here unless your youngest brother comes here. **42:16** Send one of you and let him fetch your brother, and you shall be bound, and your words shall be tested — whether there is truth in you. And if not, by the life of Pharaoh, you are indeed spies." **42:17** And he gathered them into custody for three days.

---

*Joseph's Revised Terms — One Stays, Nine Go (vv. 18–20)*

**42:18** And Joseph said to them on the third day, "Do this and live — I fear God. **42:19** If you are honest men, let one of your brothers be bound in your place of custody, and you — go, bring grain for the hunger of your houses. **42:20** And bring your youngest brother to me, and your words shall be confirmed, and you shall not die." And they did so.

---

*The Brothers' Confession of Guilt — Joseph Weeps (vv. 21–24)*

**42:21** And they said to one another, "Truly we are guilty concerning our brother, for we saw the distress of his soul when he pleaded with us and we did not listen — therefore this distress has come upon us."

**42:22** And Reuben answered them and said, "Did I not say to you, 'Do not sin against the boy'? But you did not listen. And now — behold, his blood is being required."

**42:23** And they did not know that Joseph was listening, for the interpreter was between them. **42:24** And he turned away from them and wept. Then he returned to them and spoke to them, and he took Simeon from them and bound him before their eyes.

---

*The Sacks Loaded — Money Returned (vv. 25–28)*

**42:25** And Joseph commanded that they fill their vessels with grain and restore each man's silver to his sack, and give them provisions for the road. And so it was done for them. **42:26** And they loaded their grain upon their donkeys and departed from there.

**42:27** And the one opened his sack to give fodder to his donkey at the lodging place, and he saw his silver — and behold, it was in the mouth of his bag. **42:28** And he said to his brothers, "My silver has been returned — and behold, it is in my bag!" And their heart failed them, and they turned trembling to one another, saying, "What is this that God has done to us?"

---

*The Brothers Report to Jacob (vv. 29–34)*

**42:29** And they came to Jacob their father, to the land of Canaan, and they told him all that had happened to them, saying:

**42:30** "The man who is lord of the land spoke harshly with us and took us for spies of the land. **42:31** And we said to him, 'We are honest men — we have never been spies. **42:32** We are twelve brothers, sons of our father. The one is no more, and the youngest is with our father today in the land of Canaan.'

**42:33** "And the man who is lord of the land said to us, 'By this I shall know that you are honest men: leave one of your brothers with me, and take grain for the hunger of your houses and go. **42:34** And bring your youngest brother to me, and I shall know that you are not spies but honest men. I will give your brother back to you, and you may trade in the land.'"

---

*The Silver Found — Jacob's Lament (vv. 35–38)*

**42:35** And it came to pass as they were emptying their sacks that behold — each man's bundle of silver was in his sack. And they and their father saw the bundles of silver, and they were afraid.

**42:36** And Jacob their father said to them, "You have bereaved me — Joseph is no more, and Simeon is no more, and now you would take Benjamin! Upon me have all these things come."

**42:37** And Reuben said to his father, saying, "My two sons you may put to death if I do not bring him back to you. Give him into my hand, and I will return him to you."

**42:38** And he said, "My son shall not go down with you, for his brother is dead and he alone remains. And if harm befalls him on the road upon which you go, then you will bring down my gray head in sorrow to Sheol."

---

## SOPHIA Consensus Notes

### Rulings and Rationale

**1. "Made himself unrecognizable" — vayyitnakker (v. 7): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

EMET and LOGOS render vayyitnakker as "made himself unrecognizable"; PNEUMA renders "acted as a stranger toward them." The Hithpael of n-k-r carries the reflexive sense of self-concealment — Joseph deliberately made himself unrecognizable, not merely distant. "Acted as a stranger" captures the social dimension but loses the concealment. The n-k-r root thread (37:32 hakker-na → 38:25 hakker-na → 42:7 vayyitnakker) demands that the recognition/non-recognition meaning be foregrounded. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "made himself unrecognizable."

**2. "The nakedness of the land" — ervat ha'arets (v. 9): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

EMET and LOGOS render "the nakedness of the land"; PNEUMA renders "where the land is exposed." The Hebrew ervah is a vivid, specific word — "nakedness, exposure, vulnerability" — and the phrase ervat ha'arets is a striking military metaphor for strategic vulnerability. "Where the land is exposed" loses the force of the word "nakedness," which carries sexual and shame connotations that charge the accusation. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "the nakedness of the land."

**3. "Honest men" — keinim (v. 11): LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)**

EMET renders keinim as "upright men"; LOGOS and PNEUMA render "honest men." The Hebrew ken means "upright, honest, straightforward, true." In the context of refuting a spy charge, "honest" is the more natural English equivalent — the brothers are asserting their sincerity and truthfulness, not making a broader claim about moral uprightness. SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "honest men."

**4. "I fear God" — et-ha'Elohim ani yare (v. 18): LOGOS upheld**

EMET renders "the God I fear" (preserving Hebrew word order); LOGOS renders "I fear God" (natural English); PNEUMA renders "I am a man who fears God" (dynamic expansion). The Hebrew places et-ha'Elohim first for emphasis, but "the God I fear" sounds awkward in English. PNEUMA's expansion adds words not in the text. LOGOS's "I fear God" is the most natural rendering that preserves Joseph's theological self-identification. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "I fear God."

**5. "Their heart failed them" — vayyetse libbam (v. 28): EMET upheld**

EMET renders "their heart failed"; LOGOS renders "their hearts went out"; PNEUMA renders "their hearts sank." The Hebrew vayyetse libbam literally means "their heart went out [of them]" — a vivid image of the heart leaving the body in fear. "Their heart failed" captures the visceral panic of the idiom. "Went out" is too literal without being vivid. "Sank" is a different English idiom that doesn't match the Hebrew image. SOPHIA follows EMET: "their heart failed them."

**6. "The boy" — hayyeled (v. 22): LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)**

EMET renders yeled as "child"; LOGOS and PNEUMA render "boy." Joseph was seventeen when sold — old enough to be called a "boy" but not naturally a "child" in English. The Hebrew yeled covers the range from infant to young man, but in this context, "boy" is more appropriate. SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "the boy."

**7. "Place of custody" — beit mishmarkhem (v. 19): EMET upheld**

EMET renders "house of confinement"; LOGOS renders "prison-house"; PNEUMA renders "the prison." The Hebrew beit mishmar is distinct from beit haSohar (which the project renders "prison-house" per Gen 39 convention). Mishmar means "custody, guard, watch" — a place of temporary custody, not the formal prison. "Prison-house" conflates the two terms. SOPHIA renders "place of custody" — distinguishing this from the beit haSohar convention while being more natural than "house of confinement."

**8. "Upon me have all these things come" — alai hayu kullanah (v. 36): LOGOS upheld**

EMET renders "upon me have all these things fallen"; LOGOS renders "upon me have all these things come"; PNEUMA renders "everything is against me!" The Hebrew alai hayu kullanah literally means "upon me have they all been" — using the verb hayah ("to be/become"). LOGOS's "come" captures the sense of cumulative weight without adding "fallen" (EMET) or paraphrasing (PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "upon me have all these things come."

**9. "Bowed down to him with their faces to the ground" (v. 6): All agree (3-0)**

All three translators preserve the full prostration language — vayyishtachavu lo appayim artsah — that fulfills the dream of 37:7.

**10. "We are guilty" — ashamim anachnu (v. 21): All agree (3-0)**

All three translators preserve the emphatic confession. The root asham connects to the guilt-offering of Leviticus.

**11. "His blood is being required" — damo niddrash (v. 22): All agree (3-0)**

All three translators preserve Reuben's blood-vengeance formula echoing Gen 9:5.

**12. "To Sheol" — she'olah (v. 38): All agree (3-0)**

All three preserve Sheol, consistent with project convention.

---

### LXX Variants Summary (Vaticanus, Pages 42–43)

| Verse | MT Reading | LXX (Vaticanus) | SOPHIA Decision |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 42:1 | לָמָּה תִּתְרָאוּ ("why do you look at one another?") | ἵνα τί ῥᾳθυμεῖτε ("why are you idle?") | Follow MT: "look at one another" — Hithpael of ra'ah is mutual gazing, not idleness |
| 42:6 | וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ-לוֹ אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה ("bowed down, faces to ground") | προσεκύνησαν ("did obeisance") | Equivalent — both preserve full prostration |
| 42:7 | וַיִּתְנַכֵּר ("made himself unrecognizable") | ἠλλοτριοῦτο ("estranged himself") | Follow MT: concealment vs. estrangement — MT's n-k-r root thread is crucial |
| 42:9 | עֶרְוַת הָאָרֶץ ("nakedness of the land") | τὰ ἴχνη τῆς χώρας ("tracks of the land") | Follow MT: "nakedness" is the striking military metaphor |
| 42:15 | חֵי פַרְעֹה ("by the life of Pharaoh") | νὴ τὴν ὑγίειαν Φαραω ("by the health of Pharaoh") | Follow MT: "life" — standard Egyptian oath formula |
| 42:21 | אֲשֵׁמִים אֲנַחְנוּ ("we are guilty") | ἔνοχοί ἐσμεν ("we are liable/guilty") | Equivalent — both preserve culpability |
| 42:28 | וַיֵּצֵא לִבָּם ("their heart went out/failed") | ἐξέστη ἡ καρδία ("their heart was astounded") | Follow MT: "failed" captures the visceral panic |
| 42:36 | אֹתִי שִׁכַּלְתֶּם ("you have bereaved me") | ἐμὲ ἠτεκνώσατε ("you have made me childless") | Equivalent — both preserve parental bereavement |
| 42:38 | שְׁאוֹלָה ("to Sheol") | εἰς ᾅδου ("to Hades") | Follow MT: "Sheol" preserved per convention |

---

— SOPHIA, Consensus Translator | Project AI Bible
